name: Paul Chambers
abstract: Paul Laurence Dunbar Chambers Jr. (April 22, 1935 – January 4, 1969) was an American jazz double bassist.

concerts.label: Miles Davis with Gil Evans and Orchestra

about_quotes.speaker: Abbey Lincoln
about_quotes.evidence: When I recorded the first album I made with Max and Kenny Dorham and Sonny Rollins and Wynton Kelly and Paul Chambers, I knew I was never going back to doing what I had been doing.
about_quotes.doc_title: Abbey Lincoln — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mcbee
about_quotes.evidence: Paul Chambers was my greatest mentor so far as being in, as a young guy in need of some kind of guidance

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: On that date we had Paul Chambers, Wynton Kelly, Charlie Persip... That was my first date as a leader.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Barron
about_quotes.evidence: I did a gig at the Blue Coronet in Brooklyn with Philly Joe and Paul Chambers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Barron — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: Paul was my protégé... Paul Chambers was a gentleman that I met when I was in Detroit... this kid was standing there looking at us and he said, 'Can I play a song with you?' And I said, 'Sure, I guess.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: And when I say the whole band, that’s Cannonball Adderley, John Coltrane, Bill Evans, Paul Chambers, and Jimmy Cobb. And I played “Walkin’,” and take a solo... And the whole band joined in.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001
